2284	Assert(GetPrivateRefCount(BufferDescriptorGetBuffer(buf_hdr)) == 1);
2285
2286	/* have buffer pinned, so it's safe to read tag without lock */
2287	tag = buf_hdr->tag;
2288
2289	hash = BufTableHashCode(&tag);
2290	partition_lock = BufMappingPartitionLock(hash);
2291
2292	LWLockAcquire(partition_lock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
2293
2294	/* lock the buffer header */
2295	buf_state = LockBufHdr(buf_hdr);
2296
2297	/*
2298	 * We have the buffer pinned nobody else should have been able to unset
2299	 * this concurrently.
2300	 */
2301	Assert(buf_state & BM_TAG_VALID);
2302	Assert(BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) > 0);
2303	Assert(BufferTagsEqual(&buf_hdr->tag, &tag));
2304
2305	/*
2306	 * If somebody else pinned the buffer since, or even worse, dirtied it,
2307	 * give up on this buffer: It's clearly in use.
2308	 */
2309	if (BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) != 1 || (buf_state & BM_DIRTY))
2310	{
2311		Assert(BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) > 0);
2312
2313		UnlockBufHdr(buf_hdr, buf_state);
2314		LWLockRelease(partition_lock);
2315
2316		return false;
2317	}
2318
2319	/*
2320	 * Clear out the buffer's tag and flags and usagecount.  This is not
2321	 * strictly required, as BM_TAG_VALID/BM_VALID needs to be checked before
2322	 * doing anything with the buffer. But currently it's beneficial, as the
2323	 * cheaper pre-check for several linear scans of shared buffers use the
2324	 * tag (see e.g. FlushDatabaseBuffers()).
2325	 */
2326	ClearBufferTag(&buf_hdr->tag);
2327	buf_state &= ~(BUF_FLAG_MASK | BUF_USAGECOUNT_MASK);
2328	UnlockBufHdr(buf_hdr, buf_state);
2329
2330	Assert(BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) > 0);
2331
2332	/* finally delete buffer from the buffer mapping table */
2333	BufTableDelete(&tag, hash);
2334
2335	LWLockRelease(partition_lock);
2336
2337	Assert(!(buf_state & (BM_DIRTY | BM_VALID | BM_TAG_VALID)));
2338	Assert(BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) > 0);
2339	Assert(BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(pg_atomic_read_u32(&buf_hdr->state)) > 0);
2340
2341	return true;
2342}
2343
2344static Buffer
2345GetVictimBuffer(BufferAccessStrategy strategy, IOContext io_context)
2346{
2347	BufferDesc *buf_hdr;
2348	Buffer		buf;
2349	uint32		buf_state;
2350	bool		from_ring;
2351
2352	/*
2353	 * Ensure, while the spinlock's not yet held, that there's a free refcount
2354	 * entry, and a resource owner slot for the pin.
2355	 */
2356	ReservePrivateRefCountEntry();
2357	ResourceOwnerEnlarge(CurrentResourceOwner);
2358
2359	/* we return here if a prospective victim buffer gets used concurrently */
2360again:
2361
2362	/*
2363	 * Select a victim buffer.  The buffer is returned with its header
2364	 * spinlock still held!
2365	 */
2366	buf_hdr = StrategyGetBuffer(strategy, &buf_state, &from_ring);
2367	buf = BufferDescriptorGetBuffer(buf_hdr);
2368
2369	Assert(BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) == 0);
2370
2371	/* Pin the buffer and then release the buffer spinlock */
2372	PinBuffer_Locked(buf_hdr);
2373
2374	/*
2375	 * We shouldn't have any other pins for this buffer.
2376	 */
2377	CheckBufferIsPinnedOnce(buf);
2378
2379	/*
2380	 * If the buffer was dirty, try to write it out.  There is a race
2381	 * condition here, in that someone might dirty it after we released the
2382	 * buffer header lock above, or even while we are writing it out (since
2383	 * our share-lock won't prevent hint-bit updates).  We will recheck the
2384	 * dirty bit after re-locking the buffer header.
2385	 */
2386	if (buf_state & BM_DIRTY)
2387	{
2388		LWLock	   *content_lock;
2389
2390		Assert(buf_state & BM_TAG_VALID);
2391		Assert(buf_state & BM_VALID);
2392
2393		/*
2394		 * We need a share-lock on the buffer contents to write it out (else
2395		 * we might write invalid data, eg because someone else is compacting
2396		 * the page contents while we write).  We must use a conditional lock
2397		 * acquisition here to avoid deadlock.  Even though the buffer was not
2398		 * pinned (and therefore surely not locked) when StrategyGetBuffer
2399		 * returned it, someone else could have pinned and exclusive-locked it
2400		 * by the time we get here. If we try to get the lock unconditionally,
2401		 * we'd block waiting for them; if they later block waiting for us,
2402		 * deadlock ensues. (This has been observed to happen when two
2403		 * backends are both trying to split btree index pages, and the second
2404		 * one just happens to be trying to split the page the first one got
2405		 * from StrategyGetBuffer.)
2406		 */
2407		content_lock = BufferDescriptorGetContentLock(buf_hdr);
2408		if (!LWLockConditionalAcquire(content_lock, LW_SHARED))
2409		{
2410			/*
2411			 * Someone else has locked the buffer, so give it up and loop back
2412			 * to get another one.
2413			 */
2414			UnpinBuffer(buf_hdr);
2415			goto again;
2416		}
2417
2418		/*
2419		 * If using a nondefault strategy, and writing the buffer would
2420		 * require a WAL flush, let the strategy decide whether to go ahead
2421		 * and write/reuse the buffer or to choose another victim.  We need a
2422		 * lock to inspect the page LSN, so this can't be done inside
2423		 * StrategyGetBuffer.
2424		 */
2425		if (strategy != NULL)
2426		{
2427			XLogRecPtr	lsn;
2428
2429			/* Read the LSN while holding buffer header lock */
2430			buf_state = LockBufHdr(buf_hdr);
2431			lsn = BufferGetLSN(buf_hdr);
2432			UnlockBufHdr(buf_hdr, buf_state);
2433
2434			if (XLogNeedsFlush(lsn)
2435				&& StrategyRejectBuffer(strategy, buf_hdr, from_ring))
2436			{
2437				LWLockRelease(content_lock);
2438				UnpinBuffer(buf_hdr);
2439				goto again;
2440			}
2441		}
2442
2443		/* OK, do the I/O */
2444		FlushBuffer(buf_hdr, NULL, IOOBJECT_RELATION, io_context);
2445		LWLockRelease(content_lock);
2446
2447		ScheduleBufferTagForWriteback(&BackendWritebackContext, io_context,
2448									  &buf_hdr->tag);
2449	}
2450
2451
2452	if (buf_state & BM_VALID)
2453	{
2454		/*
2455		 * When a BufferAccessStrategy is in use, blocks evicted from shared
2456		 * buffers are counted as IOOP_EVICT in the corresponding context
2457		 * (e.g. IOCONTEXT_BULKWRITE). Shared buffers are evicted by a
2458		 * strategy in two cases: 1) while initially claiming buffers for the
2459		 * strategy ring 2) to replace an existing strategy ring buffer
2460		 * because it is pinned or in use and cannot be reused.
2461		 *
2462		 * Blocks evicted from buffers already in the strategy ring are
2463		 * counted as IOOP_REUSE in the corresponding strategy context.
2464		 *
2465		 * At this point, we can accurately count evictions and reuses,
2466		 * because we have successfully claimed the valid buffer. Previously,
2467		 * we may have been forced to release the buffer due to concurrent
2468		 * pinners or erroring out.
2469		 */
2470		pgstat_count_io_op(IOOBJECT_RELATION, io_context,
2471						   from_ring ? IOOP_REUSE : IOOP_EVICT, 1, 0);
2472	}
2473
2474	/*
2475	 * If the buffer has an entry in the buffer mapping table, delete it. This
2476	 * can fail because another backend could have pinned or dirtied the
2477	 * buffer.
2478	 */
2479	if ((buf_state & BM_TAG_VALID) && !InvalidateVictimBuffer(buf_hdr))
2480	{
2481		UnpinBuffer(buf_hdr);
2482		goto again;
2483	}
2484
2485	/* a final set of sanity checks */
2486#ifdef USE_ASSERT_CHECKING
2487	buf_state = pg_atomic_read_u32(&buf_hdr->state);
2488
2489	Assert(BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) == 1);
2490	Assert(!(buf_state & (BM_TAG_VALID | BM_VALID | BM_DIRTY)));
2491
2492	CheckBufferIsPinnedOnce(buf);
2493#endif
2494
2495	return buf;
2496}
2497
2498/*
2499 * Return the maximum number of buffers that a backend should try to pin once,
2500 * to avoid exceeding its fair share.  This is the highest value that
2501 * GetAdditionalPinLimit() could ever return.  Note that it may be zero on a
2502 * system with a very small buffer pool relative to max_connections.
2503 */
2504uint32
2505GetPinLimit(void)
2506{
2507	return MaxProportionalPins;
2508}
2509
2510/*
2511 * Return the maximum number of additional buffers that this backend should
2512 * pin if it wants to stay under the per-backend limit, considering the number
2513 * of buffers it has already pinned.  Unlike LimitAdditionalPins(), the limit
2514 * return by this function can be zero.
2515 */
2516uint32
2517GetAdditionalPinLimit(void)
2518{
2519	uint32		estimated_pins_held;
2520
2521	/*
2522	 * We get the number of "overflowed" pins for free, but don't know the
2523	 * number of pins in PrivateRefCountArray.  The cost of calculating that
2524	 * exactly doesn't seem worth it, so just assume the max.
2525	 */
2526	estimated_pins_held = PrivateRefCountOverflowed + REFCOUNT_ARRAY_ENTRIES;
2527
2528	/* Is this backend already holding more than its fair share? */
2529	if (estimated_pins_held > MaxProportionalPins)
2530		return 0;
2531
2532	return MaxProportionalPins - estimated_pins_held;
2533}
2534
2535/*
2536 * Limit the number of pins a batch operation may additionally acquire, to
2537 * avoid running out of pinnable buffers.
2538 *
2539 * One additional pin is always allowed, on the assumption that the operation
2540 * requires at least one to make progress.
2541 */
2542void
2543LimitAdditionalPins(uint32 *additional_pins)
2544{
2545	uint32		limit;
2546
2547	if (*additional_pins <= 1)
2548		return;
2549
2550	limit = GetAdditionalPinLimit();
2551	limit = Max(limit, 1);
2552	if (limit < *additional_pins)
2553		*additional_pins = limit;
2554}
2555
2556/*
2557 * Logic shared between ExtendBufferedRelBy(), ExtendBufferedRelTo(). Just to
2558 * avoid duplicating the tracing and relpersistence related logic.
2559 */
2560static BlockNumber
2561ExtendBufferedRelCommon(BufferManagerRelation bmr,
2562						ForkNumber fork,
2563						BufferAccessStrategy strategy,
2564						uint32 flags,
2565						uint32 extend_by,
2566						BlockNumber extend_upto,
2567						Buffer *buffers,
2568						uint32 *extended_by)
2569{
2570	BlockNumber first_block;
2571
2572	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_EXTEND_START(fork,
2573										 bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator.locator.spcOid,
2574										 bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator.locator.dbOid,
2575										 bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator.locator.relNumber,
2576										 bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator.backend,
2577										 extend_by);
2578
2579	if (bmr.relpersistence == RELPERSISTENCE_TEMP)
2580		first_block = ExtendBufferedRelLocal(bmr, fork, flags,
2581											 extend_by, extend_upto,
2582											 buffers, &extend_by);
2583	else
2584		first_block = ExtendBufferedRelShared(bmr, fork, strategy, flags,
2585											  extend_by, extend_upto,
2586											  buffers, &extend_by);
2587	*extended_by = extend_by;
2588
2589	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_EXTEND_DONE(fork,
2590										bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator.locator.spcOid,
2591										bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator.locator.dbOid,
2592										bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator.locator.relNumber,
2593										bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator.backend,
2594										*extended_by,
2595										first_block);
2596
2597	return first_block;
2598}
2599
2600/*
2601 * Implementation of ExtendBufferedRelBy() and ExtendBufferedRelTo() for
2602 * shared buffers.
2603 */
2604static BlockNumber
2605ExtendBufferedRelShared(BufferManagerRelation bmr,
2606						ForkNumber fork,
2607						BufferAccessStrategy strategy,
2608						uint32 flags,
2609						uint32 extend_by,
2610						BlockNumber extend_upto,
2611						Buffer *buffers,
2612						uint32 *extended_by)
2613{
2614	BlockNumber first_block;
2615	IOContext	io_context = IOContextForStrategy(strategy);
2616	instr_time	io_start;
2617
2618	LimitAdditionalPins(&extend_by);
2619
2620	/*
2621	 * Acquire victim buffers for extension without holding extension lock.
2622	 * Writing out victim buffers is the most expensive part of extending the
2623	 * relation, particularly when doing so requires WAL flushes. Zeroing out
2624	 * the buffers is also quite expensive, so do that before holding the
2625	 * extension lock as well.
2626	 *
2627	 * These pages are pinned by us and not valid. While we hold the pin they
2628	 * can't be acquired as victim buffers by another backend.
2629	 */
2630	for (uint32 i = 0; i < extend_by; i++)
2631	{
2632		Block		buf_block;
2633
2634		buffers[i] = GetVictimBuffer(strategy, io_context);
2635		buf_block = BufHdrGetBlock(GetBufferDescriptor(buffers[i] - 1));
2636
2637		/* new buffers are zero-filled */
2638		MemSet(buf_block, 0, BLCKSZ);
2639	}
2640
2641	/*
2642	 * Lock relation against concurrent extensions, unless requested not to.
2643	 *
2644	 * We use the same extension lock for all forks. That's unnecessarily
2645	 * restrictive, but currently extensions for forks don't happen often
2646	 * enough to make it worth locking more granularly.
2647	 *
2648	 * Note that another backend might have extended the relation by the time
2649	 * we get the lock.
2650	 */
2651	if (!(flags & EB_SKIP_EXTENSION_LOCK))
2652		LockRelationForExtension(bmr.rel, ExclusiveLock);
2653
2654	/*
2655	 * If requested, invalidate size cache, so that smgrnblocks asks the
2656	 * kernel.
2657	 */
2658	if (flags & EB_CLEAR_SIZE_CACHE)
2659		bmr.smgr->smgr_cached_nblocks[fork] = InvalidBlockNumber;
2660
2661	first_block = smgrnblocks(bmr.smgr, fork);
2662
2663	/*
2664	 * Now that we have the accurate relation size, check if the caller wants
2665	 * us to extend to only up to a specific size. If there were concurrent
2666	 * extensions, we might have acquired too many buffers and need to release
2667	 * them.
2668	 */
2669	if (extend_upto != InvalidBlockNumber)
2670	{
2671		uint32		orig_extend_by = extend_by;
2672
2673		if (first_block > extend_upto)
2674			extend_by = 0;
2675		else if ((uint64) first_block + extend_by > extend_upto)
2676			extend_by = extend_upto - first_block;
2677
2678		for (uint32 i = extend_by; i < orig_extend_by; i++)
2679		{
2680			BufferDesc *buf_hdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buffers[i] - 1);
2681
2682			/*
2683			 * The victim buffer we acquired previously is clean and unused,
2684			 * let it be found again quickly
2685			 */
2686			StrategyFreeBuffer(buf_hdr);
2687			UnpinBuffer(buf_hdr);
2688		}
2689
2690		if (extend_by == 0)
2691		{
2692			if (!(flags & EB_SKIP_EXTENSION_LOCK))
2693				UnlockRelationForExtension(bmr.rel, ExclusiveLock);
2694			*extended_by = extend_by;
2695			return first_block;
2696		}
2697	}
2698
2699	/* Fail if relation is already at maximum possible length */
2700	if ((uint64) first_block + extend_by >= MaxBlockNumber)
2701		ereport(ERROR,
2702				(errcode(ERRCODE_PROGRAM_LIMIT_EXCEEDED),
2703				 errmsg("cannot extend relation %s beyond %u blocks",
2704						relpath(bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator, fork).str,
2705						MaxBlockNumber)));
2706
2707	/*
2708	 * Insert buffers into buffer table, mark as IO_IN_PROGRESS.
2709	 *
2710	 * This needs to happen before we extend the relation, because as soon as
2711	 * we do, other backends can start to read in those pages.
2712	 */
2713	for (uint32 i = 0; i < extend_by; i++)
2714	{
2715		Buffer		victim_buf = buffers[i];
2716		BufferDesc *victim_buf_hdr = GetBufferDescriptor(victim_buf - 1);
2717		BufferTag	tag;
2718		uint32		hash;
2719		LWLock	   *partition_lock;
2720		int			existing_id;
2721
2722		/* in case we need to pin an existing buffer below */
2723		ResourceOwnerEnlarge(CurrentResourceOwner);
2724		ReservePrivateRefCountEntry();
2725
2726		InitBufferTag(&tag, &bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator.locator, fork, first_block + i);
2727		hash = BufTableHashCode(&tag);
2728		partition_lock = BufMappingPartitionLock(hash);
2729
2730		LWLockAcquire(partition_lock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
2731
2732		existing_id = BufTableInsert(&tag, hash, victim_buf_hdr->buf_id);
2733
2734		/*
2735		 * We get here only in the corner case where we are trying to extend
2736		 * the relation but we found a pre-existing buffer. This can happen
2737		 * because a prior attempt at extending the relation failed, and
2738		 * because mdread doesn't complain about reads beyond EOF (when
2739		 * zero_damaged_pages is ON) and so a previous attempt to read a block
2740		 * beyond EOF could have left a "valid" zero-filled buffer.
2741		 * Unfortunately, we have also seen this case occurring because of
2742		 * buggy Linux kernels that sometimes return an lseek(SEEK_END) result
2743		 * that doesn't account for a recent write. In that situation, the
2744		 * pre-existing buffer would contain valid data that we don't want to
2745		 * overwrite.  Since the legitimate cases should always have left a
2746		 * zero-filled buffer, complain if not PageIsNew.
2747		 */
2748		if (existing_id >= 0)
2749		{
2750			BufferDesc *existing_hdr = GetBufferDescriptor(existing_id);
2751			Block		buf_block;
2752			bool		valid;
2753
2754			/*
2755			 * Pin the existing buffer before releasing the partition lock,
2756			 * preventing it from being evicted.
2757			 */
2758			valid = PinBuffer(existing_hdr, strategy);
2759
2760			LWLockRelease(partition_lock);
2761
2762			/*
2763			 * The victim buffer we acquired previously is clean and unused,
2764			 * let it be found again quickly
2765			 */
2766			StrategyFreeBuffer(victim_buf_hdr);
2767			UnpinBuffer(victim_buf_hdr);
2768
2769			buffers[i] = BufferDescriptorGetBuffer(existing_hdr);
2770			buf_block = BufHdrGetBlock(existing_hdr);
2771
2772			if (valid && !PageIsNew((Page) buf_block))
2773				ereport(ERROR,
2774						(errmsg("unexpected data beyond EOF in block %u of relation \"%s\"",
2775								existing_hdr->tag.blockNum,
2776								relpath(bmr.smgr->smgr_rlocator, fork).str),
2777						 errhint("This has been seen to occur with buggy kernels; consider updating your system.")));
2778
2779			/*
2780			 * We *must* do smgr[zero]extend before succeeding, else the page
2781			 * will not be reserved by the kernel, and the next P_NEW call
2782			 * will decide to return the same page.  Clear the BM_VALID bit,
2783			 * do StartBufferIO() and proceed.
2784			 *
2785			 * Loop to handle the very small possibility that someone re-sets
2786			 * BM_VALID between our clearing it and StartBufferIO inspecting
2787			 * it.
2788			 */
2789			do
2790			{
2791				uint32		buf_state = LockBufHdr(existing_hdr);
2792
2793				buf_state &= ~BM_VALID;
2794				UnlockBufHdr(existing_hdr, buf_state);
2795			} while (!StartBufferIO(existing_hdr, true, false));
2796		}
2797		else
2798		{
2799			uint32		buf_state;
2800
2801			buf_state = LockBufHdr(victim_buf_hdr);
2802
2803			/* some sanity checks while we hold the buffer header lock */
2804			Assert(!(buf_state & (BM_VALID | BM_TAG_VALID | BM_DIRTY | BM_JUST_DIRTIED)));
2805			Assert(BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) == 1);
2806
2807			victim_buf_hdr->tag = tag;
2808
2809			buf_state |= BM_TAG_VALID | BUF_USAGECOUNT_ONE;
2810			if (bmr.relpersistence == RELPERSISTENCE_PERMANENT || fork == INIT_FORKNUM)
2811				buf_state |= BM_PERMANENT;
2812
2813			UnlockBufHdr(victim_buf_hdr, buf_state);
2814
2815			LWLockRelease(partition_lock);
2816
2817			/* XXX: could combine the locked operations in it with the above */
2818			StartBufferIO(victim_buf_hdr, true, false);
2819		}
2820	}
2821
2822	io_start = pgstat_prepare_io_time(track_io_timing);
2823
2824	/*
2825	 * Note: if smgrzeroextend fails, we will end up with buffers that are
2826	 * allocated but not marked BM_VALID.  The next relation extension will
2827	 * still select the same block number (because the relation didn't get any
2828	 * longer on disk) and so future attempts to extend the relation will find
2829	 * the same buffers (if they have not been recycled) but come right back
2830	 * here to try smgrzeroextend again.
2831	 *
2832	 * We don't need to set checksum for all-zero pages.
2833	 */
2834	smgrzeroextend(bmr.smgr, fork, first_block, extend_by, false);
2835
2836	/*
2837	 * Release the file-extension lock; it's now OK for someone else to extend
2838	 * the relation some more.
2839	 *
2840	 * We remove IO_IN_PROGRESS after this, as waking up waiting backends can
2841	 * take noticeable time.
2842	 */
2843	if (!(flags & EB_SKIP_EXTENSION_LOCK))
2844		UnlockRelationForExtension(bmr.rel, ExclusiveLock);
2845
2846	pgstat_count_io_op_time(IOOBJECT_RELATION, io_context, IOOP_EXTEND,
2847							io_start, 1, extend_by * BLCKSZ);
2848
2849	/* Set BM_VALID, terminate IO, and wake up any waiters */
2850	for (uint32 i = 0; i < extend_by; i++)
2851	{
2852		Buffer		buf = buffers[i];
2853		BufferDesc *buf_hdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buf - 1);
2854		bool		lock = false;
2855
2856		if (flags & EB_LOCK_FIRST && i == 0)
2857			lock = true;
2858		else if (flags & EB_LOCK_TARGET)
2859		{
2860			Assert(extend_upto != InvalidBlockNumber);
2861			if (first_block + i + 1 == extend_upto)
2862				lock = true;
2863		}
2864
2865		if (lock)
2866			LWLockAcquire(BufferDescriptorGetContentLock(buf_hdr), LW_EXCLUSIVE);
2867
2868		TerminateBufferIO(buf_hdr, false, BM_VALID, true, false);
2869	}
2870
2871	pgBufferUsage.shared_blks_written += extend_by;
2872
2873	*extended_by = extend_by;
2874
2875	return first_block;
2876}
2877
2878/*
2879 * BufferIsExclusiveLocked
2880 *
2881 *      Checks if buffer is exclusive-locked.
2882 *
2883 * Buffer must be pinned.
2884 */
2885bool
2886BufferIsExclusiveLocked(Buffer buffer)
2887{
2888	BufferDesc *bufHdr;
2889
2890	Assert(BufferIsPinned(buffer));
2891
2892	if (BufferIsLocal(buffer))
2893	{
2894		/* Content locks are not maintained for local buffers. */
2895		return true;
2896	}
2897	else
2898	{
2899		bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buffer - 1);
2900		return LWLockHeldByMeInMode(BufferDescriptorGetContentLock(bufHdr),
2901									LW_EXCLUSIVE);
2902	}
2903}
2904
2905/*
2906 * BufferIsDirty
2907 *
2908 *		Checks if buffer is already dirty.
2909 *
2910 * Buffer must be pinned and exclusive-locked.  (Without an exclusive lock,
2911 * the result may be stale before it's returned.)
2912 */
2913bool
2914BufferIsDirty(Buffer buffer)
2915{
2916	BufferDesc *bufHdr;
2917
2918	Assert(BufferIsPinned(buffer));
2919
2920	if (BufferIsLocal(buffer))
2921	{
2922		int			bufid = -buffer - 1;
2923
2924		bufHdr = GetLocalBufferDescriptor(bufid);
2925		/* Content locks are not maintained for local buffers. */
2926	}
2927	else
2928	{
2929		bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buffer - 1);
2930		Assert(LWLockHeldByMeInMode(BufferDescriptorGetContentLock(bufHdr),
2931									LW_EXCLUSIVE));
2932	}
2933
2934	return pg_atomic_read_u32(&bufHdr->state) & BM_DIRTY;
2935}
2936
2937/*
2938 * MarkBufferDirty
2939 *
2940 *		Marks buffer contents as dirty (actual write happens later).
2941 *
2942 * Buffer must be pinned and exclusive-locked.  (If caller does not hold
2943 * exclusive lock, then somebody could be in process of writing the buffer,
2944 * leading to risk of bad data written to disk.)
2945 */
2946void
2947MarkBufferDirty(Buffer buffer)
2948{
2949	BufferDesc *bufHdr;
2950	uint32		buf_state;
2951	uint32		old_buf_state;
2952
2953	if (!BufferIsValid(buffer))
2954		elog(ERROR, "bad buffer ID: %d", buffer);
2955
2956	if (BufferIsLocal(buffer))
2957	{
2958		MarkLocalBufferDirty(buffer);
2959		return;
2960	}
2961
2962	bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buffer - 1);
2963
2964	Assert(BufferIsPinned(buffer));
2965	Assert(LWLockHeldByMeInMode(BufferDescriptorGetContentLock(bufHdr),
2966								LW_EXCLUSIVE));
2967
2968	old_buf_state = pg_atomic_read_u32(&bufHdr->state);
2969	for (;;)
2970	{
2971		if (old_buf_state & BM_LOCKED)
2972			old_buf_state = WaitBufHdrUnlocked(bufHdr);
2973
2974		buf_state = old_buf_state;
2975
2976		Assert(BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) > 0);
2977		buf_state |= BM_DIRTY | BM_JUST_DIRTIED;
2978
2979		if (pg_atomic_compare_exchange_u32(&bufHdr->state, &old_buf_state,
2980										   buf_state))
2981			break;
2982	}
2983
2984	/*
2985	 * If the buffer was not dirty already, do vacuum accounting.
2986	 */
2987	if (!(old_buf_state & BM_DIRTY))
2988	{
2989		pgBufferUsage.shared_blks_dirtied++;
2990		if (VacuumCostActive)
2991			VacuumCostBalance += VacuumCostPageDirty;
2992	}
2993}
2994
2995/*
2996 * ReleaseAndReadBuffer -- combine ReleaseBuffer() and ReadBuffer()
2997 *
2998 * Formerly, this saved one cycle of acquiring/releasing the BufMgrLock
2999 * compared to calling the two routines separately.  Now it's mainly just
3000 * a convenience function.  However, if the passed buffer is valid and
3001 * already contains the desired block, we just return it as-is; and that
3002 * does save considerable work compared to a full release and reacquire.
3003 *
3004 * Note: it is OK to pass buffer == InvalidBuffer, indicating that no old
3005 * buffer actually needs to be released.  This case is the same as ReadBuffer,
3006 * but can save some tests in the caller.
3007 */
3008Buffer
3009ReleaseAndReadBuffer(Buffer buffer,
3010					 Relation relation,
3011					 BlockNumber blockNum)
3012{
3013	ForkNumber	forkNum = MAIN_FORKNUM;
3014	BufferDesc *bufHdr;
3015
3016	if (BufferIsValid(buffer))
3017	{
3018		Assert(BufferIsPinned(buffer));
3019		if (BufferIsLocal(buffer))
3020		{
3021			bufHdr = GetLocalBufferDescriptor(-buffer - 1);
3022			if (bufHdr->tag.blockNum == blockNum &&
3023				BufTagMatchesRelFileLocator(&bufHdr->tag, &relation->rd_locator) &&
3024				BufTagGetForkNum(&bufHdr->tag) == forkNum)
3025				return buffer;
3026			UnpinLocalBuffer(buffer);
3027		}
3028		else
3029		{
3030			bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buffer - 1);
3031			/* we have pin, so it's ok to examine tag without spinlock */
3032			if (bufHdr->tag.blockNum == blockNum &&
3033				BufTagMatchesRelFileLocator(&bufHdr->tag, &relation->rd_locator) &&
3034				BufTagGetForkNum(&bufHdr->tag) == forkNum)
3035				return buffer;
3036			UnpinBuffer(bufHdr);
3037		}
3038	}
3039
3040	return ReadBuffer(relation, blockNum);
3041}
3042
3043/*
3044 * PinBuffer -- make buffer unavailable for replacement.
3045 *
3046 * For the default access strategy, the buffer's usage_count is incremented
3047 * when we first pin it; for other strategies we just make sure the usage_count
3048 * isn't zero.  (The idea of the latter is that we don't want synchronized
3049 * heap scans to inflate the count, but we need it to not be zero to discourage
3050 * other backends from stealing buffers from our ring.  As long as we cycle
3051 * through the ring faster than the global clock-sweep cycles, buffers in
3052 * our ring won't be chosen as victims for replacement by other backends.)
3053 *
3054 * This should be applied only to shared buffers, never local ones.
3055 *
3056 * Since buffers are pinned/unpinned very frequently, pin buffers without
3057 * taking the buffer header lock; instead update the state variable in loop of
3058 * CAS operations. Hopefully it's just a single CAS.
3059 *
3060 * Note that ResourceOwnerEnlarge() and ReservePrivateRefCountEntry()
3061 * must have been done already.
3062 *
3063 * Returns true if buffer is BM_VALID, else false.  This provision allows
3064 * some callers to avoid an extra spinlock cycle.
3065 */
3066static bool
3067PinBuffer(BufferDesc *buf, BufferAccessStrategy strategy)
3068{
3069	Buffer		b = BufferDescriptorGetBuffer(buf);
3070	bool		result;
3071	PrivateRefCountEntry *ref;
3072
3073	Assert(!BufferIsLocal(b));
3074	Assert(ReservedRefCountEntry != NULL);
3075
3076	ref = GetPrivateRefCountEntry(b, true);
3077
3078	if (ref == NULL)
3079	{
3080		uint32		buf_state;
3081		uint32		old_buf_state;
3082
3083		ref = NewPrivateRefCountEntry(b);
3084
3085		old_buf_state = pg_atomic_read_u32(&buf->state);
3086		for (;;)
3087		{
3088			if (old_buf_state & BM_LOCKED)
3089				old_buf_state = WaitBufHdrUnlocked(buf);
3090
3091			buf_state = old_buf_state;
3092
3093			/* increase refcount */
3094			buf_state += BUF_REFCOUNT_ONE;
3095
3096			if (strategy == NULL)
3097			{
3098				/* Default case: increase usagecount unless already max. */
3099				if (BUF_STATE_GET_USAGECOUNT(buf_state) < BM_MAX_USAGE_COUNT)
3100					buf_state += BUF_USAGECOUNT_ONE;
3101			}
3102			else
3103			{
3104				/*
3105				 * Ring buffers shouldn't evict others from pool.  Thus we
3106				 * don't make usagecount more than 1.
3107				 */
3108				if (BUF_STATE_GET_USAGECOUNT(buf_state) == 0)
3109					buf_state += BUF_USAGECOUNT_ONE;
3110			}
3111
3112			if (pg_atomic_compare_exchange_u32(&buf->state, &old_buf_state,
3113											   buf_state))
3114			{
3115				result = (buf_state & BM_VALID) != 0;
3116
3117				/*
3118				 * Assume that we acquired a buffer pin for the purposes of
3119				 * Valgrind buffer client checks (even in !result case) to
3120				 * keep things simple.  Buffers that are unsafe to access are
3121				 * not generally guaranteed to be marked undefined or
3122				 * non-accessible in any case.
3123				 */
3124				VALGRIND_MAKE_MEM_DEFINED(BufHdrGetBlock(buf), BLCKSZ);
3125				break;
3126			}
3127		}
3128	}
3129	else
3130	{
3131		/*
3132		 * If we previously pinned the buffer, it is likely to be valid, but
3133		 * it may not be if StartReadBuffers() was called and
3134		 * WaitReadBuffers() hasn't been called yet.  We'll check by loading
3135		 * the flags without locking.  This is racy, but it's OK to return
3136		 * false spuriously: when WaitReadBuffers() calls StartBufferIO(),
3137		 * it'll see that it's now valid.
3138		 *
3139		 * Note: We deliberately avoid a Valgrind client request here.
3140		 * Individual access methods can optionally superimpose buffer page
3141		 * client requests on top of our client requests to enforce that
3142		 * buffers are only accessed while locked (and pinned).  It's possible
3143		 * that the buffer page is legitimately non-accessible here.  We
3144		 * cannot meddle with that.
3145		 */
3146		result = (pg_atomic_read_u32(&buf->state) & BM_VALID) != 0;
3147	}
3148
3149	ref->refcount++;
3150	Assert(ref->refcount > 0);
3151	ResourceOwnerRememberBuffer(CurrentResourceOwner, b);
3152	return result;
3153}
3154
3155/*
3156 * PinBuffer_Locked -- as above, but caller already locked the buffer header.
3157 * The spinlock is released before return.
3158 *
3159 * As this function is called with the spinlock held, the caller has to
3160 * previously call ReservePrivateRefCountEntry() and
3161 * ResourceOwnerEnlarge(CurrentResourceOwner);
3162 *
3163 * Currently, no callers of this function want to modify the buffer's
3164 * usage_count at all, so there's no need for a strategy parameter.
3165 * Also we don't bother with a BM_VALID test (the caller could check that for
3166 * itself).
3167 *
3168 * Also all callers only ever use this function when it's known that the
3169 * buffer can't have a preexisting pin by this backend. That allows us to skip
3170 * searching the private refcount array & hash, which is a boon, because the
3171 * spinlock is still held.
3172 *
3173 * Note: use of this routine is frequently mandatory, not just an optimization
3174 * to save a spin lock/unlock cycle, because we need to pin a buffer before
3175 * its state can change under us.
3176 */
3177static void
3178PinBuffer_Locked(BufferDesc *buf)
3179{
3180	Buffer		b;
3181	PrivateRefCountEntry *ref;
3182	uint32		buf_state;
3183
3184	/*
3185	 * As explained, We don't expect any preexisting pins. That allows us to
3186	 * manipulate the PrivateRefCount after releasing the spinlock
3187	 */
3188	Assert(GetPrivateRefCountEntry(BufferDescriptorGetBuffer(buf), false) == NULL);
3189
3190	/*
3191	 * Buffer can't have a preexisting pin, so mark its page as defined to
3192	 * Valgrind (this is similar to the PinBuffer() case where the backend
3193	 * doesn't already have a buffer pin)
3194	 */
3195	VALGRIND_MAKE_MEM_DEFINED(BufHdrGetBlock(buf), BLCKSZ);
3196
3197	/*
3198	 * Since we hold the buffer spinlock, we can update the buffer state and
3199	 * release the lock in one operation.
3200	 */
3201	buf_state = pg_atomic_read_u32(&buf->state);
3202	Assert(buf_state & BM_LOCKED);
3203	buf_state += BUF_REFCOUNT_ONE;
3204	UnlockBufHdr(buf, buf_state);
3205
3206	b = BufferDescriptorGetBuffer(buf);
3207
3208	ref = NewPrivateRefCountEntry(b);
3209	ref->refcount++;
3210
3211	ResourceOwnerRememberBuffer(CurrentResourceOwner, b);
3212}
3213
3214/*
3215 * Support for waking up another backend that is waiting for the cleanup lock
3216 * to be released using BM_PIN_COUNT_WAITER.
3217 *
3218 * See LockBufferForCleanup().
3219 *
3220 * Expected to be called just after releasing a buffer pin (in a BufferDesc,
3221 * not just reducing the backend-local pincount for the buffer).
3222 */
3223static void
3224WakePinCountWaiter(BufferDesc *buf)
3225{
3226	/*
3227	 * Acquire the buffer header lock, re-check that there's a waiter. Another
3228	 * backend could have unpinned this buffer, and already woken up the
3229	 * waiter.
3230	 *
3231	 * There's no danger of the buffer being replaced after we unpinned it
3232	 * above, as it's pinned by the waiter. The waiter removes
3233	 * BM_PIN_COUNT_WAITER if it stops waiting for a reason other than this
3234	 * backend waking it up.
3235	 */
3236	uint32		buf_state = LockBufHdr(buf);
3237
3238	if ((buf_state & BM_PIN_COUNT_WAITER) &&
3239		BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) == 1)
3240	{
3241		/* we just released the last pin other than the waiter's */
3242		int			wait_backend_pgprocno = buf->wait_backend_pgprocno;
3243
3244		buf_state &= ~BM_PIN_COUNT_WAITER;
3245		UnlockBufHdr(buf, buf_state);
3246		ProcSendSignal(wait_backend_pgprocno);
3247	}
3248	else
3249		UnlockBufHdr(buf, buf_state);
3250}
3251
3252/*
3253 * UnpinBuffer -- make buffer available for replacement.
3254 *
3255 * This should be applied only to shared buffers, never local ones.  This
3256 * always adjusts CurrentResourceOwner.
3257 */
3258static void
3259UnpinBuffer(BufferDesc *buf)
3260{
3261	Buffer		b = BufferDescriptorGetBuffer(buf);
3262
3263	ResourceOwnerForgetBuffer(CurrentResourceOwner, b);
3264	UnpinBufferNoOwner(buf);
3265}
3266
3267static void
3268UnpinBufferNoOwner(BufferDesc *buf)
3269{
3270	PrivateRefCountEntry *ref;
3271	Buffer		b = BufferDescriptorGetBuffer(buf);
3272
3273	Assert(!BufferIsLocal(b));
3274
3275	/* not moving as we're likely deleting it soon anyway */
3276	ref = GetPrivateRefCountEntry(b, false);
3277	Assert(ref != NULL);
3278	Assert(ref->refcount > 0);
3279	ref->refcount--;
3280	if (ref->refcount == 0)
3281	{
3282		uint32		buf_state;
3283		uint32		old_buf_state;
3284
3285		/*
3286		 * Mark buffer non-accessible to Valgrind.
3287		 *
3288		 * Note that the buffer may have already been marked non-accessible
3289		 * within access method code that enforces that buffers are only
3290		 * accessed while a buffer lock is held.
3291		 */
3292		VALGRIND_MAKE_MEM_NOACCESS(BufHdrGetBlock(buf), BLCKSZ);
3293
3294		/* I'd better not still hold the buffer content lock */
3295		Assert(!LWLockHeldByMe(BufferDescriptorGetContentLock(buf)));
3296
3297		/*
3298		 * Decrement the shared reference count.
3299		 *
3300		 * Since buffer spinlock holder can update status using just write,
3301		 * it's not safe to use atomic decrement here; thus use a CAS loop.
3302		 */
3303		old_buf_state = pg_atomic_read_u32(&buf->state);
3304		for (;;)
3305		{
3306			if (old_buf_state & BM_LOCKED)
3307				old_buf_state = WaitBufHdrUnlocked(buf);
3308
3309			buf_state = old_buf_state;
3310
3311			buf_state -= BUF_REFCOUNT_ONE;
3312
3313			if (pg_atomic_compare_exchange_u32(&buf->state, &old_buf_state,
3314											   buf_state))
3315				break;
3316		}
3317
3318		/* Support LockBufferForCleanup() */
3319		if (buf_state & BM_PIN_COUNT_WAITER)
3320			WakePinCountWaiter(buf);
3321
3322		ForgetPrivateRefCountEntry(ref);
3323	}
3324}
3325
3326#define ST_SORT sort_checkpoint_bufferids
3327#define ST_ELEMENT_TYPE CkptSortItem
3328#define ST_COMPARE(a, b) ckpt_buforder_comparator(a, b)
3329#define ST_SCOPE static
3330#define ST_DEFINE
3331#include "lib/sort_template.h"
3332
3333/*
3334 * BufferSync -- Write out all dirty buffers in the pool.
3335 *
3336 * This is called at checkpoint time to write out all dirty shared buffers.
3337 * The checkpoint request flags should be passed in.  If CHECKPOINT_IMMEDIATE
3338 * is set, we disable delays between writes; if CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN,
3339 * CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY or CHECKPOINT_FLUSH_ALL is set, we write even
3340 * unlogged buffers, which are otherwise skipped.  The remaining flags
3341 * currently have no effect here.
3342 */
3343static void
3344BufferSync(int flags)
3345{
3346	uint32		buf_state;
3347	int			buf_id;
3348	int			num_to_scan;
3349	int			num_spaces;
3350	int			num_processed;
3351	int			num_written;
3352	CkptTsStatus *per_ts_stat = NULL;
3353	Oid			last_tsid;
3354	binaryheap *ts_heap;
3355	int			i;
3356	int			mask = BM_DIRTY;
3357	WritebackContext wb_context;
3358
3359	/*
3360	 * Unless this is a shutdown checkpoint or we have been explicitly told,
3361	 * we write only permanent, dirty buffers.  But at shutdown or end of
3362	 * recovery, we write all dirty buffers.
3363	 */
3364	if (!((flags & (CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN | CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY |
3365					CHECKPOINT_FLUSH_ALL))))
3366		mask |= BM_PERMANENT;
3367
3368	/*
3369	 * Loop over all buffers, and mark the ones that need to be written with
3370	 * BM_CHECKPOINT_NEEDED.  Count them as we go (num_to_scan), so that we
3371	 * can estimate how much work needs to be done.
3372	 *
3373	 * This allows us to write only those pages that were dirty when the
3374	 * checkpoint began, and not those that get dirtied while it proceeds.
3375	 * Whenever a page with BM_CHECKPOINT_NEEDED is written out, either by us
3376	 * later in this function, or by normal backends or the bgwriter cleaning
3377	 * scan, the flag is cleared.  Any buffer dirtied after this point won't
3378	 * have the flag set.
3379	 *
3380	 * Note that if we fail to write some buffer, we may leave buffers with
3381	 * BM_CHECKPOINT_NEEDED still set.  This is OK since any such buffer would
3382	 * certainly need to be written for the next checkpoint attempt, too.
3383	 */
3384	num_to_scan = 0;
3385	for (buf_id = 0; buf_id < NBuffers; buf_id++)
3386	{
3387		BufferDesc *bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buf_id);
3388
3389		/*
3390		 * Header spinlock is enough to examine BM_DIRTY, see comment in
3391		 * SyncOneBuffer.
3392		 */
3393		buf_state = LockBufHdr(bufHdr);
3394
3395		if ((buf_state & mask) == mask)
3396		{
3397			CkptSortItem *item;
3398
3399			buf_state |= BM_CHECKPOINT_NEEDED;
3400
3401			item = &CkptBufferIds[num_to_scan++];
3402			item->buf_id = buf_id;
3403			item->tsId = bufHdr->tag.spcOid;
3404			item->relNumber = BufTagGetRelNumber(&bufHdr->tag);
3405			item->forkNum = BufTagGetForkNum(&bufHdr->tag);
3406			item->blockNum = bufHdr->tag.blockNum;
3407		}
3408
3409		UnlockBufHdr(bufHdr, buf_state);
3410
3411		/* Check for barrier events in case NBuffers is large. */
3412		if (ProcSignalBarrierPending)
3413			ProcessProcSignalBarrier();
3414	}
3415
3416	if (num_to_scan == 0)
3417		return;					/* nothing to do */
3418
3419	WritebackContextInit(&wb_context, &checkpoint_flush_after);
3420
3421	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_SYNC_START(NBuffers, num_to_scan);
3422
3423	/*
3424	 * Sort buffers that need to be written to reduce the likelihood of random
3425	 * IO. The sorting is also important for the implementation of balancing
3426	 * writes between tablespaces. Without balancing writes we'd potentially
3427	 * end up writing to the tablespaces one-by-one; possibly overloading the
3428	 * underlying system.
3429	 */
3430	sort_checkpoint_bufferids(CkptBufferIds, num_to_scan);
3431
3432	num_spaces = 0;
3433
3434	/*
3435	 * Allocate progress status for each tablespace with buffers that need to
3436	 * be flushed. This requires the to-be-flushed array to be sorted.
3437	 */
3438	last_tsid = InvalidOid;
3439	for (i = 0; i < num_to_scan; i++)
3440	{
3441		CkptTsStatus *s;
3442		Oid			cur_tsid;
3443
3444		cur_tsid = CkptBufferIds[i].tsId;
3445
3446		/*
3447		 * Grow array of per-tablespace status structs, every time a new
3448		 * tablespace is found.
3449		 */
3450		if (last_tsid == InvalidOid || last_tsid != cur_tsid)
3451		{
3452			Size		sz;
3453
3454			num_spaces++;
3455
3456			/*
3457			 * Not worth adding grow-by-power-of-2 logic here - even with a
3458			 * few hundred tablespaces this should be fine.
3459			 */
3460			sz = sizeof(CkptTsStatus) * num_spaces;
3461
3462			if (per_ts_stat == NULL)
3463				per_ts_stat = (CkptTsStatus *) palloc(sz);
3464			else
3465				per_ts_stat = (CkptTsStatus *) repalloc(per_ts_stat, sz);
3466
3467			s = &per_ts_stat[num_spaces - 1];
3468			memset(s, 0, sizeof(*s));
3469			s->tsId = cur_tsid;
3470
3471			/*
3472			 * The first buffer in this tablespace. As CkptBufferIds is sorted
3473			 * by tablespace all (s->num_to_scan) buffers in this tablespace
3474			 * will follow afterwards.
3475			 */
3476			s->index = i;
3477
3478			/*
3479			 * progress_slice will be determined once we know how many buffers
3480			 * are in each tablespace, i.e. after this loop.
3481			 */
3482
3483			last_tsid = cur_tsid;
3484		}
3485		else
3486		{
3487			s = &per_ts_stat[num_spaces - 1];
3488		}
3489
3490		s->num_to_scan++;
3491
3492		/* Check for barrier events. */
3493		if (ProcSignalBarrierPending)
3494			ProcessProcSignalBarrier();
3495	}
3496
3497	Assert(num_spaces > 0);
3498
3499	/*
3500	 * Build a min-heap over the write-progress in the individual tablespaces,
3501	 * and compute how large a portion of the total progress a single
3502	 * processed buffer is.
3503	 */
3504	ts_heap = binaryheap_allocate(num_spaces,
3505								  ts_ckpt_progress_comparator,
3506								  NULL);
3507
3508	for (i = 0; i < num_spaces; i++)
3509	{
3510		CkptTsStatus *ts_stat = &per_ts_stat[i];
3511
3512		ts_stat->progress_slice = (float8) num_to_scan / ts_stat->num_to_scan;
3513
3514		binaryheap_add_unordered(ts_heap, PointerGetDatum(ts_stat));
3515	}
3516
3517	binaryheap_build(ts_heap);
3518
3519	/*
3520	 * Iterate through to-be-checkpointed buffers and write the ones (still)
3521	 * marked with BM_CHECKPOINT_NEEDED. The writes are balanced between
3522	 * tablespaces; otherwise the sorting would lead to only one tablespace
3523	 * receiving writes at a time, making inefficient use of the hardware.
3524	 */
3525	num_processed = 0;
3526	num_written = 0;
3527	while (!binaryheap_empty(ts_heap))
3528	{
3529		BufferDesc *bufHdr = NULL;
3530		CkptTsStatus *ts_stat = (CkptTsStatus *)
3531			DatumGetPointer(binaryheap_first(ts_heap));
3532
3533		buf_id = CkptBufferIds[ts_stat->index].buf_id;
3534		Assert(buf_id != -1);
3535
3536		bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buf_id);
3537
3538		num_processed++;
3539
3540		/*
3541		 * We don't need to acquire the lock here, because we're only looking
3542		 * at a single bit. It's possible that someone else writes the buffer
3543		 * and clears the flag right after we check, but that doesn't matter
3544		 * since SyncOneBuffer will then do nothing.  However, there is a
3545		 * further race condition: it's conceivable that between the time we
3546		 * examine the bit here and the time SyncOneBuffer acquires the lock,
3547		 * someone else not only wrote the buffer but replaced it with another
3548		 * page and dirtied it.  In that improbable case, SyncOneBuffer will
3549		 * write the buffer though we didn't need to.  It doesn't seem worth
3550		 * guarding against this, though.
3551		 */
3552		if (pg_atomic_read_u32(&bufHdr->state) & BM_CHECKPOINT_NEEDED)
3553		{
3554			if (SyncOneBuffer(buf_id, false, &wb_context) & BUF_WRITTEN)
3555			{
3556				TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_SYNC_WRITTEN(buf_id);
3557				PendingCheckpointerStats.buffers_written++;
3558				num_written++;
3559			}
3560		}
3561
3562		/*
3563		 * Measure progress independent of actually having to flush the buffer
3564		 * - otherwise writing become unbalanced.
3565		 */
3566		ts_stat->progress += ts_stat->progress_slice;
3567		ts_stat->num_scanned++;
3568		ts_stat->index++;
3569
3570		/* Have all the buffers from the tablespace been processed? */
3571		if (ts_stat->num_scanned == ts_stat->num_to_scan)
3572		{
3573			binaryheap_remove_first(ts_heap);
3574		}
3575		else
3576		{
3577			/* update heap with the new progress */
3578			binaryheap_replace_first(ts_heap, PointerGetDatum(ts_stat));
3579		}
3580
3581		/*
3582		 * Sleep to throttle our I/O rate.
3583		 *
3584		 * (This will check for barrier events even if it doesn't sleep.)
3585		 */
3586		CheckpointWriteDelay(flags, (double) num_processed / num_to_scan);
3587	}
3588
3589	/*
3590	 * Issue all pending flushes. Only checkpointer calls BufferSync(), so
3591	 * IOContext will always be IOCONTEXT_NORMAL.
3592	 */
3593	IssuePendingWritebacks(&wb_context, IOCONTEXT_NORMAL);
3594
3595	pfree(per_ts_stat);
3596	per_ts_stat = NULL;
3597	binaryheap_free(ts_heap);
3598
3599	/*
3600	 * Update checkpoint statistics. As noted above, this doesn't include
3601	 * buffers written by other backends or bgwriter scan.
3602	 */
3603	CheckpointStats.ckpt_bufs_written += num_written;
3604
3605	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_SYNC_DONE(NBuffers, num_written, num_to_scan);
3606}
3607
3608/*
3609 * BgBufferSync -- Write out some dirty buffers in the pool.
3610 *
3611 * This is called periodically by the background writer process.
3612 *
3613 * Returns true if it's appropriate for the bgwriter process to go into
3614 * low-power hibernation mode.  (This happens if the strategy clock sweep
3615 * has been "lapped" and no buffer allocations have occurred recently,
3616 * or if the bgwriter has been effectively disabled by setting
3617 * bgwriter_lru_maxpages to 0.)
3618 */
3619bool
3620BgBufferSync(WritebackContext *wb_context)
3621{
3622	/* info obtained from freelist.c */
3623	int			strategy_buf_id;
3624	uint32		strategy_passes;
3625	uint32		recent_alloc;
3626
3627	/*
3628	 * Information saved between calls so we can determine the strategy
3629	 * point's advance rate and avoid scanning already-cleaned buffers.
3630	 */
3631	static bool saved_info_valid = false;
3632	static int	prev_strategy_buf_id;
3633	static uint32 prev_strategy_passes;
3634	static int	next_to_clean;
3635	static uint32 next_passes;
3636
3637	/* Moving averages of allocation rate and clean-buffer density */
3638	static float smoothed_alloc = 0;
3639	static float smoothed_density = 10.0;
3640
3641	/* Potentially these could be tunables, but for now, not */
3642	float		smoothing_samples = 16;
3643	float		scan_whole_pool_milliseconds = 120000.0;
3644
3645	/* Used to compute how far we scan ahead */
3646	long		strategy_delta;
3647	int			bufs_to_lap;
3648	int			bufs_ahead;
3649	float		scans_per_alloc;
3650	int			reusable_buffers_est;
3651	int			upcoming_alloc_est;
3652	int			min_scan_buffers;
3653
3654	/* Variables for the scanning loop proper */
3655	int			num_to_scan;
3656	int			num_written;
3657	int			reusable_buffers;
3658
3659	/* Variables for final smoothed_density update */
3660	long		new_strategy_delta;
3661	uint32		new_recent_alloc;
3662
3663	/*
3664	 * Find out where the freelist clock sweep currently is, and how many
3665	 * buffer allocations have happened since our last call.
3666	 */
3667	strategy_buf_id = StrategySyncStart(&strategy_passes, &recent_alloc);
3668
3669	/* Report buffer alloc counts to pgstat */
3670	PendingBgWriterStats.buf_alloc += recent_alloc;
3671
3672	/*
3673	 * If we're not running the LRU scan, just stop after doing the stats
3674	 * stuff.  We mark the saved state invalid so that we can recover sanely
3675	 * if LRU scan is turned back on later.
3676	 */
3677	if (bgwriter_lru_maxpages <= 0)
3678	{
3679		saved_info_valid = false;
3680		return true;
3681	}
3682
3683	/*
3684	 * Compute strategy_delta = how many buffers have been scanned by the
3685	 * clock sweep since last time.  If first time through, assume none. Then
3686	 * see if we are still ahead of the clock sweep, and if so, how many
3687	 * buffers we could scan before we'd catch up with it and "lap" it. Note:
3688	 * weird-looking coding of xxx_passes comparisons are to avoid bogus
3689	 * behavior when the passes counts wrap around.
3690	 */
3691	if (saved_info_valid)
3692	{
3693		int32		passes_delta = strategy_passes - prev_strategy_passes;
3694
3695		strategy_delta = strategy_buf_id - prev_strategy_buf_id;
3696		strategy_delta += (long) passes_delta * NBuffers;
3697
3698		Assert(strategy_delta >= 0);
3699
3700		if ((int32) (next_passes - strategy_passes) > 0)
3701		{
3702			/* we're one pass ahead of the strategy point */
3703			bufs_to_lap = strategy_buf_id - next_to_clean;
3704#ifdef BGW_DEBUG
3705			elog(DEBUG2, "bgwriter ahead: bgw %u-%u strategy %u-%u delta=%ld lap=%d",
3706				 next_passes, next_to_clean,
3707				 strategy_passes, strategy_buf_id,
3708				 strategy_delta, bufs_to_lap);
3709#endif
3710		}
3711		else if (next_passes == strategy_passes &&
3712				 next_to_clean >= strategy_buf_id)
3713		{
3714			/* on same pass, but ahead or at least not behind */
3715			bufs_to_lap = NBuffers - (next_to_clean - strategy_buf_id);
3716#ifdef BGW_DEBUG
3717			elog(DEBUG2, "bgwriter ahead: bgw %u-%u strategy %u-%u delta=%ld lap=%d",
3718				 next_passes, next_to_clean,
3719				 strategy_passes, strategy_buf_id,
3720				 strategy_delta, bufs_to_lap);
3721#endif
3722		}
3723		else
3724		{
3725			/*
3726			 * We're behind, so skip forward to the strategy point and start
3727			 * cleaning from there.
3728			 */
3729#ifdef BGW_DEBUG
3730			elog(DEBUG2, "bgwriter behind: bgw %u-%u strategy %u-%u delta=%ld",
3731				 next_passes, next_to_clean,
3732				 strategy_passes, strategy_buf_id,
3733				 strategy_delta);
3734#endif
3735			next_to_clean = strategy_buf_id;
3736			next_passes = strategy_passes;
3737			bufs_to_lap = NBuffers;
3738		}
3739	}
3740	else
3741	{
3742		/*
3743		 * Initializing at startup or after LRU scanning had been off. Always
3744		 * start at the strategy point.
3745		 */
3746#ifdef BGW_DEBUG
3747		elog(DEBUG2, "bgwriter initializing: strategy %u-%u",
3748			 strategy_passes, strategy_buf_id);
3749#endif
3750		strategy_delta = 0;
3751		next_to_clean = strategy_buf_id;
3752		next_passes = strategy_passes;
3753		bufs_to_lap = NBuffers;
3754	}
3755
3756	/* Update saved info for next time */
3757	prev_strategy_buf_id = strategy_buf_id;
3758	prev_strategy_passes = strategy_passes;
3759	saved_info_valid = true;
3760
3761	/*
3762	 * Compute how many buffers had to be scanned for each new allocation, ie,
3763	 * 1/density of reusable buffers, and track a moving average of that.
3764	 *
3765	 * If the strategy point didn't move, we don't update the density estimate
3766	 */
3767	if (strategy_delta > 0 && recent_alloc > 0)
3768	{
3769		scans_per_alloc = (float) strategy_delta / (float) recent_alloc;
3770		smoothed_density += (scans_per_alloc - smoothed_density) /
3771			smoothing_samples;
3772	}
3773
3774	/*
3775	 * Estimate how many reusable buffers there are between the current
3776	 * strategy point and where we've scanned ahead to, based on the smoothed
3777	 * density estimate.
3778	 */
3779	bufs_ahead = NBuffers - bufs_to_lap;
3780	reusable_buffers_est = (float) bufs_ahead / smoothed_density;
3781
3782	/*
3783	 * Track a moving average of recent buffer allocations.  Here, rather than
3784	 * a true average we want a fast-attack, slow-decline behavior: we
3785	 * immediately follow any increase.
3786	 */
3787	if (smoothed_alloc <= (float) recent_alloc)
3788		smoothed_alloc = recent_alloc;
3789	else
3790		smoothed_alloc += ((float) recent_alloc - smoothed_alloc) /
3791			smoothing_samples;
3792
3793	/* Scale the estimate by a GUC to allow more aggressive tuning. */
3794	upcoming_alloc_est = (int) (smoothed_alloc * bgwriter_lru_multiplier);
3795
3796	/*
3797	 * If recent_alloc remains at zero for many cycles, smoothed_alloc will
3798	 * eventually underflow to zero, and the underflows produce annoying
3799	 * kernel warnings on some platforms.  Once upcoming_alloc_est has gone to
3800	 * zero, there's no point in tracking smaller and smaller values of
3801	 * smoothed_alloc, so just reset it to exactly zero to avoid this
3802	 * syndrome.  It will pop back up as soon as recent_alloc increases.
3803	 */
3804	if (upcoming_alloc_est == 0)
3805		smoothed_alloc = 0;
3806
3807	/*
3808	 * Even in cases where there's been little or no buffer allocation
3809	 * activity, we want to make a small amount of progress through the buffer
3810	 * cache so that as many reusable buffers as possible are clean after an
3811	 * idle period.
3812	 *
3813	 * (scan_whole_pool_milliseconds / BgWriterDelay) computes how many times
3814	 * the BGW will be called during the scan_whole_pool time; slice the
3815	 * buffer pool into that many sections.
3816	 */
3817	min_scan_buffers = (int) (NBuffers / (scan_whole_pool_milliseconds / BgWriterDelay));
3818
3819	if (upcoming_alloc_est < (min_scan_buffers + reusable_buffers_est))
3820	{
3821#ifdef BGW_DEBUG
3822		elog(DEBUG2, "bgwriter: alloc_est=%d too small, using min=%d + reusable_est=%d",
3823			 upcoming_alloc_est, min_scan_buffers, reusable_buffers_est);
3824#endif
3825		upcoming_alloc_est = min_scan_buffers + reusable_buffers_est;
3826	}
3827
3828	/*
3829	 * Now write out dirty reusable buffers, working forward from the
3830	 * next_to_clean point, until we have lapped the strategy scan, or cleaned
3831	 * enough buffers to match our estimate of the next cycle's allocation
3832	 * requirements, or hit the bgwriter_lru_maxpages limit.
3833	 */
3834
3835	num_to_scan = bufs_to_lap;
3836	num_written = 0;
3837	reusable_buffers = reusable_buffers_est;
3838
3839	/* Execute the LRU scan */
3840	while (num_to_scan > 0 && reusable_buffers < upcoming_alloc_est)
3841	{
3842		int			sync_state = SyncOneBuffer(next_to_clean, true,
3843											   wb_context);
3844
3845		if (++next_to_clean >= NBuffers)
3846		{
3847			next_to_clean = 0;
3848			next_passes++;
3849		}
3850		num_to_scan--;
3851
3852		if (sync_state & BUF_WRITTEN)
3853		{
3854			reusable_buffers++;
3855			if (++num_written >= bgwriter_lru_maxpages)
3856			{
3857				PendingBgWriterStats.maxwritten_clean++;
3858				break;
3859			}
3860		}
3861		else if (sync_state & BUF_REUSABLE)
3862			reusable_buffers++;
3863	}
3864
3865	PendingBgWriterStats.buf_written_clean += num_written;
3866
3867#ifdef BGW_DEBUG
3868	elog(DEBUG1, "bgwriter: recent_alloc=%u smoothed=%.2f delta=%ld ahead=%d density=%.2f reusable_est=%d upcoming_est=%d scanned=%d wrote=%d reusable=%d",
3869		 recent_alloc, smoothed_alloc, strategy_delta, bufs_ahead,
3870		 smoothed_density, reusable_buffers_est, upcoming_alloc_est,
3871		 bufs_to_lap - num_to_scan,
3872		 num_written,
3873		 reusable_buffers - reusable_buffers_est);
3874#endif
3875
3876	/*
3877	 * Consider the above scan as being like a new allocation scan.
3878	 * Characterize its density and update the smoothed one based on it. This
3879	 * effectively halves the moving average period in cases where both the
3880	 * strategy and the background writer are doing some useful scanning,
3881	 * which is helpful because a long memory isn't as desirable on the
3882	 * density estimates.
3883	 */
3884	new_strategy_delta = bufs_to_lap - num_to_scan;
3885	new_recent_alloc = reusable_buffers - reusable_buffers_est;
3886	if (new_strategy_delta > 0 && new_recent_alloc > 0)
3887	{
3888		scans_per_alloc = (float) new_strategy_delta / (float) new_recent_alloc;
3889		smoothed_density += (scans_per_alloc - smoothed_density) /
3890			smoothing_samples;
3891
3892#ifdef BGW_DEBUG
3893		elog(DEBUG2, "bgwriter: cleaner density alloc=%u scan=%ld density=%.2f new smoothed=%.2f",
3894			 new_recent_alloc, new_strategy_delta,
3895			 scans_per_alloc, smoothed_density);
3896#endif
3897	}
3898
3899	/* Return true if OK to hibernate */
3900	return (bufs_to_lap == 0 && recent_alloc == 0);
3901}
3902
3903/*
3904 * SyncOneBuffer -- process a single buffer during syncing.
3905 *
3906 * If skip_recently_used is true, we don't write currently-pinned buffers, nor
3907 * buffers marked recently used, as these are not replacement candidates.
3908 *
3909 * Returns a bitmask containing the following flag bits:
3910 *	BUF_WRITTEN: we wrote the buffer.
3911 *	BUF_REUSABLE: buffer is available for replacement, ie, it has
3912 *		pin count 0 and usage count 0.
3913 *
3914 * (BUF_WRITTEN could be set in error if FlushBuffer finds the buffer clean
3915 * after locking it, but we don't care all that much.)
3916 */
3917static int
3918SyncOneBuffer(int buf_id, bool skip_recently_used, WritebackContext *wb_context)
3919{
3920	BufferDesc *bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buf_id);
3921	int			result = 0;
3922	uint32		buf_state;
3923	BufferTag	tag;
3924
3925	/* Make sure we can handle the pin */
3926	ReservePrivateRefCountEntry();
3927	ResourceOwnerEnlarge(CurrentResourceOwner);
3928
3929	/*
3930	 * Check whether buffer needs writing.
3931	 *
3932	 * We can make this check without taking the buffer content lock so long
3933	 * as we mark pages dirty in access methods *before* logging changes with
3934	 * XLogInsert(): if someone marks the buffer dirty just after our check we
3935	 * don't worry because our checkpoint.redo points before log record for
3936	 * upcoming changes and so we are not required to write such dirty buffer.
3937	 */
3938	buf_state = LockBufHdr(bufHdr);
3939
3940	if (BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state) == 0 &&
3941		BUF_STATE_GET_USAGECOUNT(buf_state) == 0)
3942	{
3943		result |= BUF_REUSABLE;
3944	}
3945	else if (skip_recently_used)
3946	{
3947		/* Caller told us not to write recently-used buffers */
3948		UnlockBufHdr(bufHdr, buf_state);
3949		return result;
3950	}
3951
3952	if (!(buf_state & BM_VALID) || !(buf_state & BM_DIRTY))
3953	{
3954		/* It's clean, so nothing to do */
3955		UnlockBufHdr(bufHdr, buf_state);
3956		return result;
3957	}
3958
3959	/*
3960	 * Pin it, share-lock it, write it.  (FlushBuffer will do nothing if the
3961	 * buffer is clean by the time we've locked it.)
3962	 */
3963	PinBuffer_Locked(bufHdr);
3964	LWLockAcquire(BufferDescriptorGetContentLock(bufHdr), LW_SHARED);
3965
3966	FlushBuffer(bufHdr, NULL, IOOBJECT_RELATION, IOCONTEXT_NORMAL);
3967
3968	LWLockRelease(BufferDescriptorGetContentLock(bufHdr));
3969
3970	tag = bufHdr->tag;
3971
3972	UnpinBuffer(bufHdr);
3973
3974	/*
3975	 * SyncOneBuffer() is only called by checkpointer and bgwriter, so
3976	 * IOContext will always be IOCONTEXT_NORMAL.
3977	 */
3978	ScheduleBufferTagForWriteback(wb_context, IOCONTEXT_NORMAL, &tag);
3979
3980	return result | BUF_WRITTEN;
3981}
3982
3983/*
3984 *		AtEOXact_Buffers - clean up at end of transaction.
3985 *
3986 *		As of PostgreSQL 8.0, buffer pins should get released by the
3987 *		ResourceOwner mechanism.  This routine is just a debugging
3988 *		cross-check that no pins remain.
3989 */
3990void
3991AtEOXact_Buffers(bool isCommit)
3992{
3993	CheckForBufferLeaks();
3994
3995	AtEOXact_LocalBuffers(isCommit);
3996
3997	Assert(PrivateRefCountOverflowed == 0);
3998}
3999
4000/*
4001 * Initialize access to shared buffer pool
4002 *
4003 * This is called during backend startup (whether standalone or under the
4004 * postmaster).  It sets up for this backend's access to the already-existing
4005 * buffer pool.
4006 */
4007void
4008InitBufferManagerAccess(void)
4009{
4010	HASHCTL		hash_ctl;
4011
4012	/*
4013	 * An advisory limit on the number of pins each backend should hold, based
4014	 * on shared_buffers and the maximum number of connections possible.
4015	 * That's very pessimistic, but outside toy-sized shared_buffers it should
4016	 * allow plenty of pins.  LimitAdditionalPins() and
4017	 * GetAdditionalPinLimit() can be used to check the remaining balance.
4018	 */
4019	MaxProportionalPins = NBuffers / (MaxBackends + NUM_AUXILIARY_PROCS);
4020
4021	memset(&PrivateRefCountArray, 0, sizeof(PrivateRefCountArray));
4022
4023	hash_ctl.keysize = sizeof(int32);
4024	hash_ctl.entrysize = sizeof(PrivateRefCountEntry);
4025
4026	PrivateRefCountHash = hash_create("PrivateRefCount", 100, &hash_ctl,
4027									  HASH_ELEM | HASH_BLOBS);
4028
4029	/*
4030	 * AtProcExit_Buffers needs LWLock access, and thereby has to be called at
4031	 * the corresponding phase of backend shutdown.
4032	 */
4033	Assert(MyProc != NULL);
4034	on_shmem_exit(AtProcExit_Buffers, 0);
4035}
4036
4037/*
4038 * During backend exit, ensure that we released all shared-buffer locks and
4039 * assert that we have no remaining pins.
4040 */
4041static void
4042AtProcExit_Buffers(int code, Datum arg)
4043{
4044	UnlockBuffers();
4045
4046	CheckForBufferLeaks();
4047
4048	/* localbuf.c needs a chance too */
4049	AtProcExit_LocalBuffers();
4050}
4051
4052/*
4053 *		CheckForBufferLeaks - ensure this backend holds no buffer pins
4054 *
4055 *		As of PostgreSQL 8.0, buffer pins should get released by the
4056 *		ResourceOwner mechanism.  This routine is just a debugging
4057 *		cross-check that no pins remain.
4058 */
4059static void
4060CheckForBufferLeaks(void)
4061{
4062#ifdef USE_ASSERT_CHECKING
4063	int			RefCountErrors = 0;
4064	PrivateRefCountEntry *res;
4065	int			i;
4066	char	   *s;
4067
4068	/* check the array */
4069	for (i = 0; i < REFCOUNT_ARRAY_ENTRIES; i++)
4070	{
4071		res = &PrivateRefCountArray[i];
4072
4073		if (res->buffer != InvalidBuffer)
4074		{
4075			s = DebugPrintBufferRefcount(res->buffer);
4076			elog(WARNING, "buffer refcount leak: %s", s);
4077			pfree(s);
4078
4079			RefCountErrors++;
4080		}
4081	}
4082
4083	/* if necessary search the hash */
4084	if (PrivateRefCountOverflowed)
4085	{
4086		HASH_SEQ_STATUS hstat;
4087
4088		hash_seq_init(&hstat, PrivateRefCountHash);
4089		while ((res = (PrivateRefCountEntry *) hash_seq_search(&hstat)) != NULL)
4090		{
4091			s = DebugPrintBufferRefcount(res->buffer);
4092			elog(WARNING, "buffer refcount leak: %s", s);
4093			pfree(s);
4094			RefCountErrors++;
4095		}
4096	}
4097
4098	Assert(RefCountErrors == 0);
4099#endif
4100}
4101
4102#ifdef USE_ASSERT_CHECKING
4103/*
4104 * Check for exclusive-locked catalog buffers.  This is the core of
4105 * AssertCouldGetRelation().
4106 *
4107 * A backend would self-deadlock on LWLocks if the catalog scan read the
4108 * exclusive-locked buffer.  The main threat is exclusive-locked buffers of
4109 * catalogs used in relcache, because a catcache search on any catalog may
4110 * build that catalog's relcache entry.  We don't have an inventory of
4111 * catalogs relcache uses, so just check buffers of most catalogs.
4112 *
4113 * It's better to minimize waits while holding an exclusive buffer lock, so it
4114 * would be nice to broaden this check not to be catalog-specific.  However,
4115 * bttextcmp() accesses pg_collation, and non-core opclasses might similarly
4116 * read tables.  That is deadlock-free as long as there's no loop in the
4117 * dependency graph: modifying table A may cause an opclass to read table B,
4118 * but it must not cause a read of table A.
4119 */
4120void
4121AssertBufferLocksPermitCatalogRead(void)
4122{
4123	ForEachLWLockHeldByMe(AssertNotCatalogBufferLock, NULL);
4124}
4125
4126static void
4127AssertNotCatalogBufferLock(LWLock *lock, LWLockMode mode,
4128						   void *unused_context)
4129{
4130	BufferDesc *bufHdr;
4131	BufferTag	tag;
4132	Oid			relid;
4133
4134	if (mode != LW_EXCLUSIVE)
4135		return;
4136
4137	if (!((BufferDescPadded *) lock > BufferDescriptors &&
4138		  (BufferDescPadded *) lock < BufferDescriptors + NBuffers))
4139		return;					/* not a buffer lock */
4140
4141	bufHdr = (BufferDesc *)
4142		((char *) lock - offsetof(BufferDesc, content_lock));
4143	tag = bufHdr->tag;
4144
4145	/*
4146	 * This relNumber==relid assumption holds until a catalog experiences
4147	 * VACUUM FULL or similar.  After a command like that, relNumber will be
4148	 * in the normal (non-catalog) range, and we lose the ability to detect
4149	 * hazardous access to that catalog.  Calling RelidByRelfilenumber() would
4150	 * close that gap, but RelidByRelfilenumber() might then deadlock with a
4151	 * held lock.
4152	 */
4153	relid = tag.relNumber;
4154
4155	if (IsCatalogTextUniqueIndexOid(relid)) /* see comments at the callee */
4156		return;
4157
4158	Assert(!IsCatalogRelationOid(relid));
4159}
4160#endif
4161
4162
4163/*
4164 * Helper routine to issue warnings when a buffer is unexpectedly pinned
4165 */
4166char *
4167DebugPrintBufferRefcount(Buffer buffer)
4168{
4169	BufferDesc *buf;
4170	int32		loccount;
4171	char	   *result;
4172	ProcNumber	backend;
4173	uint32		buf_state;
4174
4175	Assert(BufferIsValid(buffer));
4176	if (BufferIsLocal(buffer))
4177	{
4178		buf = GetLocalBufferDescriptor(-buffer - 1);
4179		loccount = LocalRefCount[-buffer - 1];
4180		backend = MyProcNumber;
4181	}
4182	else
4183	{
4184		buf = GetBufferDescriptor(buffer - 1);
4185		loccount = GetPrivateRefCount(buffer);
4186		backend = INVALID_PROC_NUMBER;
4187	}
4188
4189	/* theoretically we should lock the bufhdr here */
4190	buf_state = pg_atomic_read_u32(&buf->state);
4191
4192	result = psprintf("[%03d] (rel=%s, blockNum=%u, flags=0x%x, refcount=%u %d)",
4193					  buffer,
4194					  relpathbackend(BufTagGetRelFileLocator(&buf->tag), backend,
4195									 BufTagGetForkNum(&buf->tag)).str,
4196					  buf->tag.blockNum, buf_state & BUF_FLAG_MASK,
4197					  BUF_STATE_GET_REFCOUNT(buf_state), loccount);
4198	return result;
4199}
4200
4201/*
4202 * CheckPointBuffers
4203 *
4204 * Flush all dirty blocks in buffer pool to disk at checkpoint time.
4205 *
4206 * Note: temporary relations do not participate in checkpoints, so they don't
4207 * need to be flushed.
4208 */
4209void
4210CheckPointBuffers(int flags)
4211{
4212	BufferSync(flags);
4213}
4214
4215/*
4216 * BufferGetBlockNumber
4217 *		Returns the block number associated with a buffer.
4218 *
4219 * Note:
4220 *		Assumes that the buffer is valid and pinned, else the
4221 *		value may be obsolete immediately...
4222 */
4223BlockNumber
4224BufferGetBlockNumber(Buffer buffer)
4225{
4226	BufferDesc *bufHdr;
4227
4228	Assert(BufferIsPinned(buffer));
4229
4230	if (BufferIsLocal(buffer))
4231		bufHdr = GetLocalBufferDescriptor(-buffer - 1);
4232	else
4233		bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buffer - 1);
4234
4235	/* pinned, so OK to read tag without spinlock */
4236	return bufHdr->tag.blockNum;
4237}
4238
4239/*
4240 * BufferGetTag
4241 *		Returns the relfilelocator, fork number and block number associated with
4242 *		a buffer.
4243 */
4244void
4245BufferGetTag(Buffer buffer, RelFileLocator *rlocator, ForkNumber *forknum,
4246			 BlockNumber *blknum)
4247{
4248	BufferDesc *bufHdr;
4249
4250	/* Do the same checks as BufferGetBlockNumber. */
4251	Assert(BufferIsPinned(buffer));
4252
4253	if (BufferIsLocal(buffer))
4254		bufHdr = GetLocalBufferDescriptor(-buffer - 1);
4255	else
4256		bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buffer - 1);
4257
4258	/* pinned, so OK to read tag without spinlock */
4259	*rlocator = BufTagGetRelFileLocator(&bufHdr->tag);
4260	*forknum = BufTagGetForkNum(&bufHdr->tag);
4261	*blknum = bufHdr->tag.blockNum;
4262}
4263
4264/*
4265 * FlushBuffer
4266 *		Physically write out a shared buffer.
4267 *
4268 * NOTE: this actually just passes the buffer contents to the kernel; the
4269 * real write to disk won't happen until the kernel feels like it.  This
4270 * is okay from our point of view since we can redo the changes from WAL.
4271 * However, we will need to force the changes to disk via fsync before
4272 * we can checkpoint WAL.
4273 *
4274 * The caller must hold a pin on the buffer and have share-locked the
4275 * buffer contents.  (Note: a share-lock does not prevent updates of
4276 * hint bits in the buffer, so the page could change while the write
4277 * is in progress, but we assume that that will not invalidate the data
4278 * written.)
4279 *
4280 * If the caller has an smgr reference for the buffer's relation, pass it
4281 * as the second parameter.  If not, pass NULL.
4282 */
4283static void
4284FlushBuffer(BufferDesc *buf, SMgrRelation reln, IOObject io_object,
4285			IOContext io_context)
4286{
4287	XLogRecPtr	recptr;
4288	ErrorContextCallback errcallback;
4289	instr_time	io_start;
4290	Block		bufBlock;
4291	char	   *bufToWrite;
4292	uint32		buf_state;
4293
4294	/*
4295	 * Try to start an I/O operation.  If StartBufferIO returns false, then
4296	 * someone else flushed the buffer before we could, so we need not do
4297	 * anything.
4298	 */
4299	if (!StartBufferIO(buf, false, false))
4300		return;
4301
4302	/* Setup error traceback support for ereport() */
4303	errcallback.callback = shared_buffer_write_error_callback;
4304	errcallback.arg = buf;
4305	errcallback.previous = error_context_stack;
4306	error_context_stack = &errcallback;
4307
4308	/* Find smgr relation for buffer */
4309	if (reln == NULL)
4310		reln = smgropen(BufTagGetRelFileLocator(&buf->tag), INVALID_PROC_NUMBER);
4311
4312	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_FLUSH_START(BufTagGetForkNum(&buf->tag),
4313										buf->tag.blockNum,
4314										reln->smgr_rlocator.locator.spcOid,
4315										reln->smgr_rlocator.locator.dbOid,
4316										reln->smgr_rlocator.locator.relNumber);
4317
4318	buf_state = LockBufHdr(buf);
4319
4320	/*
4321	 * Run PageGetLSN while holding header lock, since we don't have the
4322	 * buffer locked exclusively in all cases.
4323	 */
4324	recptr = BufferGetLSN(buf);
4325
4326	/* To check if block content changes while flushing. - vadim 01/17/97 */
4327	buf_state &= ~BM_JUST_DIRTIED;
4328	UnlockBufHdr(buf, buf_state);
4329
4330	/*
4331	 * Force XLOG flush up to buffer's LSN.  This implements the basic WAL
4332	 * rule that log updates must hit disk before any of the data-file changes
4333	 * they describe do.
4334	 *
4335	 * However, this rule does not apply to unlogged relations, which will be
4336	 * lost after a crash anyway.  Most unlogged relation pages do not bear
4337	 * LSNs since we never emit WAL records for them, and therefore flushing
4338	 * up through the buffer LSN would be useless, but harmless.  However,
4339	 * GiST indexes use LSNs internally to track page-splits, and therefore
4340	 * unlogged GiST pages bear "fake" LSNs generated by
4341	 * GetFakeLSNForUnloggedRel.  It is unlikely but possible that the fake
4342	 * LSN counter could advance past the WAL insertion point; and if it did
4343	 * happen, attempting to flush WAL through that location would fail, with
4344	 * disastrous system-wide consequences.  To make sure that can't happen,
4345	 * skip the flush if the buffer isn't permanent.
4346	 */
4347	if (buf_state & BM_PERMANENT)
4348		XLogFlush(recptr);
4349
4350	/*
4351	 * Now it's safe to write the buffer to disk. Note that no one else should
4352	 * have been able to write it, while we were busy with log flushing,
4353	 * because we got the exclusive right to perform I/O by setting the
4354	 * BM_IO_IN_PROGRESS bit.
4355	 */
4356	bufBlock = BufHdrGetBlock(buf);
4357
4358	/*
4359	 * Update page checksum if desired.  Since we have only shared lock on the
4360	 * buffer, other processes might be updating hint bits in it, so we must
4361	 * copy the page to private storage if we do checksumming.
4362	 */
4363	bufToWrite = PageSetChecksumCopy((Page) bufBlock, buf->tag.blockNum);
4364
4365	io_start = pgstat_prepare_io_time(track_io_timing);
4366
4367	/*
4368	 * bufToWrite is either the shared buffer or a copy, as appropriate.
4369	 */
4370	smgrwrite(reln,
4371			  BufTagGetForkNum(&buf->tag),
4372			  buf->tag.blockNum,
4373			  bufToWrite,
4374			  false);
4375
4376	/*
4377	 * When a strategy is in use, only flushes of dirty buffers already in the
4378	 * strategy ring are counted as strategy writes (IOCONTEXT
4379	 * [BULKREAD|BULKWRITE|VACUUM] IOOP_WRITE) for the purpose of IO
4380	 * statistics tracking.
4381	 *
4382	 * If a shared buffer initially added to the ring must be flushed before
4383	 * being used, this is counted as an IOCONTEXT_NORMAL IOOP_WRITE.
4384	 *
4385	 * If a shared buffer which was added to the ring later because the
4386	 * current strategy buffer is pinned or in use or because all strategy
4387	 * buffers were dirty and rejected (for BAS_BULKREAD operations only)
4388	 * requires flushing, this is counted as an IOCONTEXT_NORMAL IOOP_WRITE
4389	 * (from_ring will be false).
4390	 *
4391	 * When a strategy is not in use, the write can only be a "regular" write
4392	 * of a dirty shared buffer (IOCONTEXT_NORMAL IOOP_WRITE).
4393	 */
4394	pgstat_count_io_op_time(IOOBJECT_RELATION, io_context,
4395							IOOP_WRITE, io_start, 1, BLCKSZ);
4396
4397	pgBufferUsage.shared_blks_written++;
4398
4399	/*
4400	 * Mark the buffer as clean (unless BM_JUST_DIRTIED has become set) and
4401	 * end the BM_IO_IN_PROGRESS state.
4402	 */
4403	TerminateBufferIO(buf, true, 0, true, false);
4404
4405	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_FLUSH_DONE(BufTagGetForkNum(&buf->tag),
4406									   buf->tag.blockNum,
4407									   reln->smgr_rlocator.locator.spcOid,
4408									   reln->smgr_rlocator.locator.dbOid,
4409									   reln->smgr_rlocator.locator.relNumber);
4410
4411	/* Pop the error context stack */
4412	error_context_stack = errcallback.previous;
4413}
4414
4415/*
4416 * RelationGetNumberOfBlocksInFork
4417 *		Determines the current number of pages in the specified relation fork.
4418 *
4419 * Note that the accuracy of the result will depend on the details of the
4420 * relation's storage. For builtin AMs it'll be accurate, but for external AMs
4421 * it might not be.
4422 */
4423BlockNumber
4424RelationGetNumberOfBlocksInFork(Relation relation, ForkNumber forkNum)
4425{
4426	if (RELKIND_HAS_TABLE_AM(relation->rd_rel->relkind))
4427	{
4428		/*
4429		 * Not every table AM uses BLCKSZ wide fixed size blocks. Therefore
4430		 * tableam returns the size in bytes - but for the purpose of this
4431		 * routine, we want the number of blocks. Therefore divide, rounding
4432		 * up.
4433		 */
4434		uint64		szbytes;
4435
4436		szbytes = table_relation_size(relation, forkNum);
4437
4438		return (szbytes + (BLCKSZ - 1)) / BLCKSZ;
4439	}
4440	else if (RELKIND_HAS_STORAGE(relation->rd_rel->relkind))
4441	{
4442		return smgrnblocks(RelationGetSmgr(relation), forkNum);
4443	}
4444	else
4445		Assert(false);
4446
4447	return 0;					/* keep compiler quiet */
4448}
4449
4450/*
4451 * BufferIsPermanent
4452 *		Determines whether a buffer will potentially still be around after
4453 *		a crash.  Caller must hold a buffer pin.
4454 */
4455bool
4456BufferIsPermanent(Buffer buffer)
4457{
4458	BufferDesc *bufHdr;
4459
4460	/* Local buffers are used only for temp relations. */
4461	if (BufferIsLocal(buffer))
4462		return false;
4463
4464	/* Make sure we've got a real buffer, and that we hold a pin on it. */
4465	Assert(BufferIsValid(buffer));
4466	Assert(BufferIsPinned(buffer));
4467
4468	/*
4469	 * BM_PERMANENT can't be changed while we hold a pin on the buffer, so we
4470	 * need not bother with the buffer header spinlock.  Even if someone else
4471	 * changes the buffer header state while we're doing this, the state is
4472	 * changed atomically, so we'll read the old value or the new value, but
4473	 * not random garbage.
4474	 */
4475	bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buffer - 1);
4476	return (pg_atomic_read_u32(&bufHdr->state) & BM_PERMANENT) != 0;
4477}
4478
4479/*
4480 * BufferGetLSNAtomic
4481 *		Retrieves the LSN of the buffer atomically using a buffer header lock.
4482 *		This is necessary for some callers who may not have an exclusive lock
4483 *		on the buffer.
4484 */
4485XLogRecPtr
4486BufferGetLSNAtomic(Buffer buffer)
4487{
4488	char	   *page = BufferGetPage(buffer);
4489	BufferDesc *bufHdr;
4490	XLogRecPtr	lsn;
4491	uint32		buf_state;
4492
4493	/*
4494	 * If we don't need locking for correctness, fastpath out.
4495	 */
4496	if (!XLogHintBitIsNeeded() || BufferIsLocal(buffer))
4497		return PageGetLSN(page);
4498
4499	/* Make sure we've got a real buffer, and that we hold a pin on it. */
4500	Assert(BufferIsValid(buffer));
4501	Assert(BufferIsPinned(buffer));
4502
4503	bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(buffer - 1);
4504	buf_state = LockBufHdr(bufHdr);
4505	lsn = PageGetLSN(page);
4506	UnlockBufHdr(bufHdr, buf_state);
4507
4508	return lsn;
4509}
4510
4511/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
4512 *		DropRelationBuffers
4513 *
4514 *		This function removes from the buffer pool all the pages of the
4515 *		specified relation forks that have block numbers >= firstDelBlock.
4516 *		(In particular, with firstDelBlock = 0, all pages are removed.)
4517 *		Dirty pages are simply dropped, without bothering to write them
4518 *		out first.  Therefore, this is NOT rollback-able, and so should be
4519 *		used only with extreme caution!
4520 *
4521 *		Currently, this is called only from smgr.c when the underlying file
4522 *		is about to be deleted or truncated (firstDelBlock is needed for
4523 *		the truncation case).  The data in the affected pages would therefore
4524 *		be deleted momentarily anyway, and there is no point in writing it.
4525 *		It is the responsibility of higher-level code to ensure that the
4526 *		deletion or truncation does not lose any data that could be needed
4527 *		later.  It is also the responsibility of higher-level code to ensure
4528 *		that no other process could be trying to load more pages of the
4529 *		relation into buffers.
4530 * --------------------------------------------------------------------
4531 */
4532void
4533DropRelationBuffers(SMgrRelation smgr_reln, ForkNumber *forkNum,
4534					int nforks, BlockNumber *firstDelBlock)
4535{
4536	int			i;
4537	int			j;
4538	RelFileLocatorBackend rlocator;
4539	BlockNumber nForkBlock[MAX_FORKNUM];
4540	uint64		nBlocksToInvalidate = 0;
4541
4542	rlocator = smgr_reln->smgr_rlocator;
4543
4544	/* If it's a local relation, it's localbuf.c's problem. */
4545	if (RelFileLocatorBackendIsTemp(rlocator))
4546	{
4547		if (rlocator.backend == MyProcNumber)
4548		{
4549			for (j = 0; j < nforks; j++)
4550				DropRelationLocalBuffers(rlocator.locator, forkNum[j],
4551										 firstDelBlock[j]);
4552		}
4553		return;
4554	}
4555
4556	/*
4557	 * To remove all the pages of the specified relation forks from the buffer
4558	 * pool, we need to scan the entire buffer pool but we can optimize it by
4559	 * finding the buffers from BufMapping table provided we know the exact
4560	 * size of each fork of the relation. The exact size is required to ensure
4561	 * that we don't leave any buffer for the relation being dropped as
4562	 * otherwise the background writer or checkpointer can lead to a PANIC
4563	 * error while flushing buffers corresponding to files that don't exist.
4564	 *
4565	 * To know the exact size, we rely on the size cached for each fork by us
4566	 * during recovery which limits the optimization to recovery and on
4567	 * standbys but we can easily extend it once we have shared cache for
4568	 * relation size.
4569	 *
4570	 * In recovery, we cache the value returned by the first lseek(SEEK_END)
4571	 * and the future writes keeps the cached value up-to-date. See
4572	 * smgrextend. It is possible that the value of the first lseek is smaller
4573	 * than the actual number of existing blocks in the file due to buggy
4574	 * Linux kernels that might not have accounted for the recent write. But
4575	 * that should be fine because there must not be any buffers after that
4576	 * file size.
4577	 */
4578	for (i = 0; i < nforks; i++)
4579	{
4580		/* Get the number of blocks for a relation's fork */
4581		nForkBlock[i] = smgrnblocks_cached(smgr_reln, forkNum[i]);
4582
4583		if (nForkBlock[i] == InvalidBlockNumber)
4584		{
4585			nBlocksToInvalidate = InvalidBlockNumber;
4586			break;
4587		}
4588
4589		/* calculate the number of blocks to be invalidated */
4590		nBlocksToInvalidate += (nForkBlock[i] - firstDelBlock[i]);
4591	}
4592
4593	/*
4594	 * We apply the optimization iff the total number of blocks to invalidate
4595	 * is below the BUF_DROP_FULL_SCAN_THRESHOLD.
4596	 */
4597	if (BlockNumberIsValid(nBlocksToInvalidate) &&
4598		nBlocksToInvalidate < BUF_DROP_FULL_SCAN_THRESHOLD)
4599	{
4600		for (j = 0; j < nforks; j++)
4601			FindAndDropRelationBuffers(rlocator.locator, forkNum[j],
4602									   nForkBlock[j], firstDelBlock[j]);
4603		return;
4604	}
4605
4606	for (i = 0; i < NBuffers; i++)
4607	{
4608		BufferDesc *bufHdr = GetBufferDescriptor(i);
4609		uint32		buf_state;
4610
4611		/*
4612		 * We can make this a tad faster by prechecking the buffer tag before
4613		 * we attempt to lock the buffer; this saves a lot of lock
4614		 * acquisitions in typical cases.  It should be safe because the
